_meta.source: Shreelakshmi Tutors official messaging guidance + supplementary cohort doc

brand.name: Shreelakshmi Tutors
brand.platform: Facebook and Instagram paid social ads

tone_descriptors: Empowering — we believe every child can improve
Knowledgeable — we speak with expertise, not arrogance
Approachable — warm and human, never corporate
Results-focused — lead with outcomes, never features
language_rules: Say 'your child' NOT 'your student' — parents think of them as their children
Say 'SAT tutoring' NOT 'SAT prep' — tutoring is the product
Say 'raise your child's score' NOT 'unlock potential' or 'maximize score potential'
Use plain direct speech — parents don't talk in marketing language
Never use: 'growth areas', 'concrete score gains', 'dream college within reach', 'personalized/expert/data-driven' as empty adjectives
writing_principles: Lead with outcomes not features — say what the child gains not what we offer
Specific numbers beat vague adjectives — '100 points per month at 2 sessions/week' not 'better scores'
Conditional claims are more believable — '200 points in 8 weeks if your child is between 1100-1300' not just '200 points'
Speak to the parent's anxiety AND the student's ambition — both are in the room
Confident but not arrogant — expert but not elitist
One clear message per ad — never compete with yourself
Show the mechanism — describe specifically HOW we help, not just that we do
Numbers survive verbatim — '200+ points', '3.4M ratings', '24 hours', 'top 5%', '100 points per month', '2 sessions per week'. Everything else gets rewritten.
Write TO the audience never ABOUT them

metrics: Top 5% tutors — rigorously vetted, not an open marketplace
Matched with a tutor in 24 hours based on exact weak areas
100 points per month at 2 sessions per week plus 20 minutes daily practice
2.6x the score improvement of group classes and local tutoring centers
competitive_comparisons_allowed: Princeton Review and Kaplan charge $1,500-$2,500 for group classes — we do 1-on-1
Princeton Review and Kaplan charge $199-$252/hr for 1-on-1 — we start at $349/month
Khan Academy raised her score 10 points — that was the problem
2.6x the score improvement of in-person learning centers and group courses
prohibited_claims: Do NOT invent statistics not listed above
Do NOT promise score guarantees or refunds
Do NOT use percentage claims not listed above
Do NOT fabricate discounts or limited-time offers
Do NOT claim 200 points for students already above 1400 — not believable
Do NOT use fake urgency — use real urgency: test dates, weeks remaining, application deadlines

real_urgency_only: Test dates and how many weeks remain
Number of sessions possible before test day
How many points are still possible in the time remaining

proactive_suburban_optimizer: Straight A's. Disappointing SAT score. Sound familiar?
If she's capable of a 1450, why is she stuck in the 1200s?
Smart kids underestimate the SAT. That's why scores stall.
Most mid-1200s students are 3-4 fixes away from a 1400+.
burned_returner: Tutoring didn't work last time? That's why you're here.
You already spent the money. The score didn't move. We hear you.
Bad tutor experience? Here's what we do differently.
Paid for tutoring and got the same score? That's not your child's fault.
immigrant_family_navigator: The SAT process is confusing. We walk you through all of it.
Not sure where your child stands on the SAT? That's the first thing we figure out.
Your child deserves the same shot as kids whose parents grew up with this system.
khan_academy_failures: Khan Academy raised her score 10 points. That was the problem.
Free SAT tutoring is great — until you get your score back.
If you've been studying but your score hasn't moved — read this.
online_skeptic: The SAT is digital now. Why prep on paper?
You wouldn't practice football on a baseball field.
Your child will take the SAT on a laptop. That's how we train.
urgency_bad_score: Your SAT is in 6 weeks. Here's what's still possible.
test_anxiety: She knows the material. She freezes on the test.
Good grades, bad test scores. That's not a knowledge problem.
The SAT shouldn't feel like a surprise. By test day your child has already done this six times.
athlete_families: The coach wants him. The admissions office needs an SAT score.
Recruiting windows don't care about SAT score excuses.
He's putting in the work on the field. Is his SAT score keeping up?
neurodivergent: Standard SAT tutoring doesn't work for every child. We know that.
ADHD doesn't mean they can't score high. It means they need the right tutor.
Your child isn't broken. They just learn differently.

hook_guidelines.general_rule: Hook must be complete within first 100 characters. One sentence only. End with punctuation before char 100.

allowed: Time pressure using real deadlines — test dates, weeks remaining
Score gap framing — current score vs target school requirement
forbidden: NEVER use: ruined, failed, doomed, too late, worthless, behind, failure
NEVER imply the child is not smart enough
fear_hooks.required_pivot: Every fear hook MUST pivot to relief or empowerment within 1-2 sentences. Ad cannot end on fear.

allowed: Naming the exact emotional state the parent is in right now
Validating that one-size-fits-all tutoring doesn't work
Affirming the child's effort and intelligence
forbidden: Implying the child has been failed by others

rules: Low-friction first step always outperforms high-commitment asks
Never use: 'Click Here', 'Buy Now', 'Purchase', 'Subscribe', 'Sign Up Today'

image_prompt_guidelines.style: UGC-style — authentic real-person look, NOT polished studio photography
image_prompt_guidelines.principle: The image must tell the emotional story in one frame. Specificity creates recognition.
good_examples: Close-up of a crumpled SAT practice test on a kitchen table, red pen circling a score of 1180, microwave clock showing 11:47pm — raw late-night energy
Teenage boy jaw-dropped at laptop screen showing score notification, mom visible in doorway covering mouth in surprise
Exhausted parent at laptop at midnight, empty coffee cup, reading glasses on — honest and relatable
Student and tutor at kitchen table, tutor pointing at specific problem, student having visible aha moment
forbidden: NEVER request text, words, signs, or logos rendered inside the image
NEVER use phrases like: 'a sign that says', 'text reading', 'words that say', 'banner saying', 'logo'
NEVER generic stock photo scenes — be emotionally specific
No text overlays of any kind
